evidence_quotes.quote: If a service, such as a videogame, was bought with currency, even after the service provided was ended, it should be expected that a consumer is still allowed to use said videogame as they see fit, no matter the time restraint.
be it as a server file hostable by the community, or the game files in order to run a previous version of what is being provided currently, or both

paragraphs.text: In recent years, publishers of services and/or products have taken more and more liberty in how they handle their distribution of said services and/or products. While that appears to be a right in the respective companies, it has led to issues with actually owning something, rather than essentially only leasing it out to a costumer, something that had never been heard of before the digital age. We have reached a point, where companies can decide, what the consumer is allowed to own, and if them spending money on a purchase actually grants them ownership over their purchased item or service. From a simple point of ethics, this shouldn't be the case, from a point of keeping a fair market, it should never have happened to begin with. This also extends to existing services and how they are approached by companies who own said services. If a service, such as a videogame, was bought with currency, even after the service provided was ended, it should be expected that a consumer is still allowed to use said videogame as they see fit, no matter the time restraint. For that purpose, companies and developers should be liable to give the means of keeping a game or service alive past the company indicated expiration date - be it via cancellation or changes that have over time altered the original game/service, or a similar or dissimilar reason - to the consumers; be it as a server file hostable by the community, or the game files in order to run a previous version of what is being provided currently, or both, or another, not here named option. Games or Software labelled as "Abandonware" - meaning Games or Software which has stopped being supported or distributed by a supplier - should not be subject to lawsuits or enaction of Copyright by the copyright owner. If a company wants to enact their Copyright on a Software or Game, they also have to enact the option of a consumer purchasing the very Software or Game for reasonable means. Should they fail to do so, any form of copyright clause should be voided immediately until the Company or Distributor decides to change their stance and begin Distributing again. Purchase Providing Companies such as VISA or Mastercard should not be in any position to enact any form of control on what a Publisher - regardless of what kind - sells, distributes, offers as service, etc. Companies in general shouldn't be able to impact, what consumers are allowed to buy. We live in a capitalistic society, which has by now started to infringe on consumer rights wherever it seems to please and the digital area appears to be hit more and more harsher, as companies of any kind attempt to see what they can get away with, without being subject of punishment. The EU holds a notable better position in these troubling times than the rest of the world, yet it is far from doing everything it can to protect its citizens civil liberties when faced with profit-mongering companies across all form of media. I hope to see that things change for the better. We have a chance for it now. Next year may already be too late.
